cover.body_html:
10亿美元。半年15倍。

截至2026年7月，智谱AI的ARR（年度经常性收入）已经突破10亿美元。这个数字意味着什么？它意味着智谱成为了中国第一家跨入「10亿美金ARR俱乐部」的大模型公司，也意味着中国大模型行业，终于跑出了一个能靠自身业务造血的标杆。

半年前的2026年初，智谱的ARR还不到7000万美元。短短六个半月，翻了15倍。这个增长速度，放在整个SaaS历史上都是炸裂的——哪怕是当年的Slack、Zoom，在同等体量下也没有这么猛的增速。

更重要的是信号意义。过去两年，市场一直在质疑：中国大模型公司到底能不能赚到钱？是靠政府补贴撑着，还是真的有客户愿意掏钱？智谱用10亿美元ARR给出了一个响亮的回答：能，而且能赚得很快。

15倍增长从哪来：三条增长曲线同时爆发

15倍不是靠单一业务拉起来的。拆解智谱的收入结构，你会看到三条增长曲线同时在往上翘。

第一条曲线：API调用收入，基本盘快速膨胀。这是最基础也是最稳定的一块——企业开发者调用GLM系列模型的API费用。过去半年，国内大模型API价格战打得轰轰烈烈，各家轮番降价，但智谱的API收入反而在涨。为什么？因为用量增长的速度远超价格下降的速度。当企业从「试点用AI」变成「全流程用AI」，调用量是指数级上升的。价格降了50%，但用量涨了10倍，总收入还是翻5倍。

第二条曲线：行业解决方案，客单价飙升。这是智谱今年真正的杀手锏。不再只卖API，而是深入金融、政务、能源、制造等垂直行业，提供「模型+场景+交付」的一体化解决方案。这种项目的客单价不是几万几十万，而是几百万甚至上千万。而且一旦进去了，续费率极高——企业把核心业务流程建在你上面，换模型的成本比你涨价还高。

第三条曲线：C端产品矩阵，用户付费觉醒。很多人忽略了智谱在C端的布局。智谱清言的会员订阅、AI助手、教育产品……这些C端业务单看每一项都不算大，但加起来是一个不小的盘子。而且C端有一个B端比不了的优势：现金流极好——先付费后服务，没有账期问题。

三条曲线同时发力，API做规模、解决方案做利润、C端做现金流。这就是为什么智谱能在半年内跑出15倍增长——不是单点突破，是体系化的商业能力爆发。

"大模型的竞争，上半场比谁模型强，下半场比谁卖得好。模型能力会趋同，但商业化能力是真正的护城河。"—— 一位AI行业投资人

为什么是智谱？不是最早的，也不是最强的，但跑得最稳

论技术，智谱不一定是国内最强的——月之暗面的Kimi 3据说要追平Claude Opus 4.8，参数规模达到2-3万亿；百度的文心一言用户基数更大；字节的豆包在C端风生水起。

论融资，智谱也不是融钱最多的——智谱最近一轮募资314亿港元，估值约2000亿人民币；而字节、百度这些大厂本身就不缺钱。

但为什么第一个跑到10亿美金ARR的是智谱？三个原因。

第一，To B基因深厚，从一开始就瞄准企业付费。智谱的创始团队来自清华，做企业服务出身，懂客户、懂销售、懂交付。很多大模型公司是技术团队创业，满脑子都是「我模型有多强」，而智谱从一开始想的就是「客户为什么愿意付钱」。这种思维方式的差异，在商业化阶段会被无限放大。

第二，不打价格战的底线思维。过去半年大模型价格战最凶的时候，很多公司是「你敢降我就敢跟」，比谁更有耐心烧钱。但智谱的策略很明确：可以降，但不赔本赚吆喝。它的定价策略始终在「有竞争力」和「有利润」之间找平衡，不追求最便宜，但追求性价比最高。事实证明，企业选大模型供应商，价格只是决策因素之一——稳定性、安全性、服务能力，这些都比便宜几块钱重要。

第三，生态布局早，绑定了一批ISV和渠道伙伴。智谱不是自己一个人去啃所有行业客户，而是早早建了合作伙伴生态——系统集成商、ISV、咨询公司、渠道代理商。这些伙伴帮它把触角伸到了它自己覆盖不到的地方。当行业解决方案的收入起来的时候，你会发现每一个项目背后都有合作伙伴的影子。

说到底，大模型的商业化不是一个技术问题，是一个组织能力问题。你的销售体系能不能打？你的交付团队能不能扛？你的渠道生态建起来没有？这些「脏活累活」，技术出身的团队往往看不上，但它们才是商业化真正的壁垒。

行业拐点：从「比谁模型大」到「比谁收入多」

智谱10亿美金ARR这个节点，放在整个中国大模型行业的坐标系里看，是一个明确的拐点。

2023年，行业比的是「谁参数多」——你1000亿，我3000亿，他万亿参数，参数越多越牛逼。那是技术叙事时代。

2024-2025年，比的是「谁用户多」——你1亿月活，我2亿日活，用户量就是正义。那是用户叙事时代。

到了2026年下半年，叙事变了。市场开始问一个更尖锐的问题：你赚了多少钱？

这个问题一问出来，很多公司就露怯了。用户量再大，收不上钱都是虚的；参数再多，没有客户买单都是自嗨。资本的耐心是有限的——前两年可以听你讲「先做规模再变现」的故事，到了第三年第四年，你得拿出真金白银的收入来。

智谱的10亿美金ARR，相当于给整个行业打了个样：大模型不是只有OpenAI能赚钱，中国公司也能。而且不是靠政府输血，是靠实打实的企业付费和个人订阅。

这会带来一个连锁反应：投资人会更看重商业化数据，而不是技术指标。下一轮融资的时候，你再说「我模型超越GPT-5」没用了，投资人会问你「ARR多少？续费率多少？净收入留存率多少？」答不上来的，估值就要打折。

但隐忧依然存在：10亿之后，增长能持续吗？

10亿美元ARR是个里程碑，但不是终点。真正的问题是：15倍增长能持续吗？明年还能翻几倍？

几个隐忧值得关注。

第一，大客户依赖风险。智谱的收入里，政府和国企项目占了不小的比例。这类项目的特点是：单量大、利润高，但可持续性要看政策风向。如果明年财政收紧、AI相关预算压缩，收入会不会断崖式下跌？这是所有To G/To 国企业务都要面对的问题。

第二，竞争加剧，价格战可能从API蔓延到解决方案。现在大家还在比谁模型好、谁服务好，但如果越来越多的公司涌入行业解决方案市场，价格战一定会打起来——你报500万，我报300万，他报200万还包三年运维。到时候利润率能不能保住，就要打个问号。

第三，出海是必选项，但也是高风险项。国内市场再大也有天花板，10亿之后要奔100亿，必须出海。但海外市场是OpenAI、Anthropic的主场，智谱的GLM在海外的品牌认知度几乎为零。怎么打？是靠性价比打东南亚中东市场，还是硬碰硬去欧美？这是智谱接下来必须回答的战略命题。

还有一个更深层的问题：大模型公司的终局到底是什么？是像Oracle那样的企业软件巨头？是像AWS那样的云服务提供商？还是像Google那样的广告+搜索公司？没人知道答案，因为这个行业太新了，连OpenAI都还在摸索。

终局判断：商业化能力，才是大模型公司的真正壁垒

智谱的10亿美金ARR，是中国大模型行业的一个成人礼。

它告诉所有人：这个赛道不是只有投入没有产出，不是只有概念没有收入，不是只有补贴没有真金白银。只要你真的能帮客户解决问题，客户是愿意掏钱的，而且愿意掏很多钱。

但它也敲响了警钟：模型能力的红利期快结束了。再过一两年，头部几家大模型公司的能力差距会缩小到用户感知不到的程度——就像今天的云服务，AWS、Azure、GCP，用起来差别没那么大。到那时候，真正决定胜负的，不是谁模型更强，而是谁卖得更好、谁交付更快、谁生态更牢、谁客户更忠诚。

这就是为什么智谱的15倍增长值得被认真对待——它不是一个孤立的公司新闻，是整个行业的风向标。大模型的竞争，正在从「技术竞赛」切换到「商业竞赛」。上半场靠技术赢的公司，下半场不一定能继续赢。

10亿美元ARR只是一个开始。接下来的两年，我们会看到中国大模型行业的第一次大洗牌——有人掉队，有人突围，有人被收购，有人冲上市。而洗牌的标准，不再是参数和跑分，是收入和利润。

潮水的方向变了。从今天起，别再问「谁模型最强」，要问「谁最能赚钱」。

明天见。
cover.body_html_en:
$1 billion. 15x in six months.

As of July 2026, Zhipu AI's ARR (Annual Recurring Revenue) has crossed $1 billion. What does this number mean? It means Zhipu has become China's first LLM company to join the "$1B ARR club," and it means China's LLM industry finally has a benchmark that can generate real revenue on its own.

Six months ago, at the start of 2026, Zhipu's ARR was less than $70 million. In just six and a half months, it grew 15x. That growth rate is explosive even by SaaS historical standards — even Slack and Zoom at similar scales never grew this fast.

What matters more is the signal. For the past two years, the market has been asking: can Chinese LLM companies actually make money? Are they propped up by government subsidies, or do real customers pay? Zhipu's $1B ARR delivers a resounding answer: yes, and they can make it fast.

Where the 15x Growth Came From: Three Curves Firing Simultaneously

15x growth isn't driven by a single business line. Break down Zhipu's revenue structure and you see three growth curves all tilting upward at once.

Curve one: API call revenue — the base expanding rapidly. This is the most fundamental and stable piece — what enterprise developers pay to call GLM series model APIs. Over the past six months, the domestic LLM API price war has been fierce, with everyone slashing prices in turn. Yet Zhipu's API revenue is growing. Why? Because usage is growing far faster than prices are falling. When enterprises shift from "piloting AI" to "using AI across all workflows," call volume rises exponentially. Prices drop 50%, but usage grows 10x — total revenue still quintuples.

Curve two: industry solutions — average deal size soaring. This is Zhipu's real secret weapon this year. Instead of just selling APIs, it's going deep into vertical industries — finance, government, energy, manufacturing — delivering integrated "model + scenario + implementation" solutions. These projects don't cost tens or hundreds of thousands — they're millions, even tens of millions of dollars. And once you're in, renewal rates are extremely high — when a customer builds core business processes on your platform, switching costs are higher than your price increase.

Curve three: consumer product matrix — user payment awakening. Many people overlook Zhipu's consumer-facing portfolio. Zhipu Qingyan's premium subscriptions, AI assistants, education products — none of these are huge individually, but together they're a meaningful slice. And consumer has one advantage enterprise can't match: fantastic cash flow — pay first, use later, no accounts receivable issues.

Three curves firing together — APIs for scale, solutions for margin, consumer for cash flow. That's why Zhipu can deliver 15x growth in six months — not a single-point breakthrough, but a systematic commercial capability explosion.

"In the LLM competition, the first half is about who has the better model. The second half is about who sells better. Model capabilities will converge — but commercialization ability is the real moat."— An AI industry investor

Why Zhipu? Not the First, Not the Strongest — But the Steadiest

On technology, Zhipu isn't necessarily China's strongest — Moonshot's Kimi 3 is reportedly set to match Claude Opus 4.8, with 2-3 trillion parameters. Baidu's Ernie has a larger user base. ByteDance's Doubao is killing it on the consumer side.

On funding, Zhipu isn't the most well-capitalized either — Zhipu's latest round raised HK$31.4 billion at a ~200 billion RMB valuation; companies like ByteDance and Baidu simply don't have money problems.

So why is Zhipu the first to hit $1B ARR? Three reasons.

First: deep B2B DNA, targeting enterprise payments from day one. Zhipu's founding team comes from Tsinghua with enterprise services backgrounds — they understand customers, sales, and delivery. Many LLM companies are founded by technical teams obsessed with "how strong my model is," while Zhipu has been thinking from the start about "why would customers pay?" This mindset difference gets amplified exponentially during the commercialization phase.

Second: bottom-line thinking — no suicidal price wars. During the fiercest LLM price wars of the past six months, many companies played "you drop, I drop more" — a game of chicken about who can burn money longer. But Zhipu's strategy was clear: we'll cut prices, but we won't lose money for market share. Its pricing has always balanced competitiveness with profitability — not the cheapest, but the best value. And it turns out enterprises choose LLM vendors based on more than just price — stability, security, service quality all matter more than saving a few bucks.

Third: early ecosystem play, building a network of ISVs and channel partners. Zhipu didn't try to win every industry customer by itself — it built a partner ecosystem early on: system integrators, ISVs, consulting firms, channel resellers. These partners extend its reach into places it couldn't cover alone. When industry solution revenue takes off, you'll find partners behind every project.

At the end of the day, LLM commercialization isn't a technology problem — it's an organizational capability problem. Can your sales organization execute? Can your delivery team scale? Have you built a channel ecosystem? Technical teams often dismiss this as "dirty work" — but it's the real moat in commercialization.

Industry Inflection: From "Who Has the Bigger Model" to "Who Makes More Money"

Zhipu's $1B ARR milestone, viewed against China's entire LLM industry, is a clear inflection point.

In 2023, the industry compared "who has more parameters" — you have 100B, I have 300B, they have a trillion — more parameters = more impressive. That was the technology narrative era.

In 2024–2025, it was "who has more users" — you have 100M MAU, I have 200M DAU, user volume is justice. That was the user narrative era.

By the second half of 2026, the narrative has shifted. The market is asking a sharper question: how much money do you make?

Ask that question and many companies fold. However big your user base, if you can't monetize it, it's hollow. However many parameters you have, if nobody pays for it, it's self-gratification. Capital has limited patience — the first two years you can tell the "scale first, monetize later" story. By year three or four, you need to show real revenue.

Zhipu's $1B ARR sets an example for the whole industry: LLM companies don't just burn money — they can make money too. And not from government subsidies — from real enterprise payments and consumer subscriptions.

This triggers a chain reaction: investors will value commercialization metrics more than technical metrics. In the next funding round, saying "my model surpasses GPT-5" won't cut it. Investors will ask, "What's your ARR? What's your renewal rate? What's your net revenue retention?" Companies that can't answer will see their valuations discounted.

But Concerns Remain: After $1B, Can Growth Continue?

$1B ARR is a milestone, but not the destination. The real question is: can 15x growth continue? How many more doublings are left?

Several concerns are worth watching.

First: large-customer concentration risk. A significant portion of Zhipu's revenue comes from government and state-owned enterprise projects. These projects have high deal sizes and good margins — but sustainability depends on policy winds. If fiscal tightening next year compresses AI budgets, could revenue drop off a cliff? This is a challenge every company with heavy G2G/G2E exposure faces.

Second: intensifying competition — price wars could spread from APIs to solutions. Right now the competition is still about model quality and service quality. But as more companies flood into the industry solutions market, price wars will inevitably break out — you quote $700K, I quote $400K, someone else quotes $280K with three years of free maintenance. When that happens, maintaining margins becomes questionable.

Third: going global is necessary — but high risk. However big the domestic market, it has a ceiling. To get from $1B to $10B, you must go overseas. But overseas is OpenAI and Anthropic's home turf — Zhipu's GLM has virtually zero brand recognition abroad. How to compete? Underdogging into Southeast Asia and the Middle East on price? Or going head-to-head in the US and Europe? That's the strategic question Zhipu must answer next.

And there's a deeper question: what is the endgame for an LLM company? An enterprise software giant like Oracle? A cloud provider like AWS? An advertising+search company like Google? Nobody knows — the industry is too new, not even OpenAI has figured it out.

Verdict: Commercialization Ability Is the Real Moat

Zhipu's $1B ARR is a coming-of-age moment for China's LLM industry.

It tells everyone: this track isn't all input and no output, isn't all concept and no revenue, isn't all subsidy and no real money. If you actually solve customer problems, customers will pay — and pay well.

But it also sounds an alarm: the model capability dividend is ending. In another year or two, the capability gap between top LLM companies will shrink to imperceptible levels — just like cloud services today, where AWS, Azure, GCP don't feel that different in practice. When that happens, what really determines winners isn't who has the better model — it's who sells better, who delivers faster, whose ecosystem is stronger, whose customers are more loyal.

That's why Zhipu's 15x growth deserves serious attention — it's not an isolated company news item, it's an industry weather vane. LLM competition is shifting from "technology race" to "commercial race." Companies that won the first half on technology won't necessarily keep winning the second half.

$1B ARR is just the beginning. Over the next two years, we'll see China's first great LLM industry shakeout — some will fall behind, some will break through, some will get acquired, some will IPO. And the metric for the shakeout won't be parameters or benchmarks. It'll be revenue and profit.

The tide has turned. From today on, stop asking "who has the strongest model." Ask "who makes the most money."

See you tomorrow.

环球网 - 苹果智能完成国内备案，接入阿里千问和百度

briefs.body_html:
等了一年多，国行iPhone用户终于能用苹果的AI功能了。

7月15日，网信部门发布备案名单，苹果智能（Apple Intelligence）正式完成端侧生成式AI服务备案。同批过审的还有华为、OPPO、vivo、努比亚等7款手机端侧AI服务——但最受关注的还是苹果。

原因很简单：这是苹果第一次在核心AI功能上明确使用第三方大模型。国行版Apple Intelligence将接入阿里巴巴旗下通义千问的部分能力，同时百度也提供了部分技术支持。那个「所有核心技术必须自研」的苹果，在中国市场妥协了。

为什么是阿里和百度？不是一个，是两个

苹果选合作伙伴的思路很有意思：不是all in一家，而是同时用两家。

据传阿里千问主要提供文本处理、图像生成和Siri增强的核心能力，百度则负责搜索增强和部分知识问答。双供应商策略，这是苹果的老传统了——就像它的芯片不只用台积电、屏幕不只用三星一样，关键供应链永远不能单点依赖。

更深层的考量是风险分散。中国的AI监管环境变化快，如果只用一家，万一那家出了问题，苹果整个AI功能都要停摆。用两家，东边不亮西边亮，至少能保证基础服务不中断。

对阿里和百度来说，这也是一个巨大的胜利。能成为苹果的供应商，本身就是一种技术背书——而且还是「国家级认证」的背书。更实际的好处是：数以亿计的国行iPhone用户，每天都在调用你的模型。这个调用量级别的训练数据和反馈，是花多少钱都买不来的。

妥协的不是技术，是市场

很多人说「苹果不行了，连AI都要靠别人」。这个判断太表面了。

苹果不是做不出来AI模型——Apple Intelligence的核心模型是苹果自己的，只是针对中国市场做了「本地化适配」。苹果的妥协，不是技术层面的，是商业和监管层面的。

中国市场对AI内容有特殊的监管要求：数据必须在境内、内容必须符合国内规范、模型必须通过安全评估。如果苹果坚持只用自己的海外模型，要么过不了审，要么体验差到没法用。与其给中国用户一个阉割版的AI功能，不如找本地合作伙伴，给用户一个完整的体验。

这不是苹果第一次这么干了。在中国，苹果的云上贵州数据合作、Apple Pay接入支付宝微信、App Store的内容审核……每一项都是「全球战略」对「中国市场特殊性」的妥协。

"苹果的中国策略从来不是『要不要妥协』，而是『妥协到什么程度才能既保住原则又保住市场』。"—— Dawn Vision编辑部

但这次妥协的分量不一样。以前的妥协都是「边缘业务」——数据存储、支付、内容审核。这次不一样，AI是下一代计算的核心入口。把核心入口的一部分交给第三方供应商，这在苹果历史上是罕见的。

这也从侧面说明了一件事：中国市场太重要了，重要到苹果愿意在核心战略上让步。iPhone在中国有几亿用户，中国的供应链支撑了苹果90%以上的产能。如果因为AI功能缺失导致中国用户换机意愿下降，那损失不是节省一点研发成本能补回来的。

明天见。
briefs.body_html_en:
After waiting over a year, Chinese iPhone users finally get Apple's AI features.

On July 15, Chinese cyberspace regulators released a filing list — Apple Intelligence officially completed its on-device generative AI service registration. Six other phone AI services also passed, from Huawei, OPPO, vivo, and Nubia — but Apple was the one everyone watched.

The reason is simple: this is the first time Apple has explicitly used third-party LLMs for a core AI feature. The China version of Apple Intelligence will integrate some capabilities from Alibaba's Tongyi Qwen, with Baidu also providing partial technical support. The Apple that "everything core must be built in-house" has compromised in the China market.

Why Alibaba and Baidu? Not One, But Two

Apple's approach to choosing partners is interesting: not all-in on one, but using two simultaneously.

Reportedly, Alibaba's Qwen provides core capabilities for text processing, image generation, and Siri enhancement, while Baidu handles search augmentation and some knowledge Q&A. Dual-supplier strategy is classic Apple — just like it doesn't use only TSMC for chips or only Samsung for displays, critical supply chains never have single points of failure.

The deeper consideration is risk diversification. China's AI regulatory environment changes fast. If Apple used only one provider and that provider ran into issues, its entire AI functionality would grind to a halt. With two, there's always a backup — at minimum, basic services stay online.

For Alibaba and Baidu, this is also a huge win. Becoming an Apple supplier is itself a technical endorsement — and a "nationally certified" one at that. The more practical benefit: hundreds of millions of Chinese iPhone users calling your model every single day. That volume of training data and feedback can't be bought at any price.

The Compromise Isn't About Technology — It's About Market

Many people say "Apple is washed up, it can't even do AI on its own." That's a surface-level take.

It's not that Apple can't build AI models — Apple Intelligence's core model is Apple's own, just with "localization adaptations" for the China market. Apple's compromise isn't technical — it's commercial and regulatory.

China has specific regulatory requirements for AI content: data must stay within the country, content must comply with domestic standards, models must pass security assessments. If Apple insisted on using only its overseas models, it either wouldn't pass review or the experience would be too poor to ship. Rather than giving Chinese users a crippled AI feature, better to find local partners and deliver a complete experience.

This isn't the first time Apple has done this. In China, Apple's Guizhou Cloud data partnership, Apple Pay integrating Alipay and WeChat Pay, App Store content moderation — every one is a compromise of "global strategy" to "China market specifics."

"Apple's China strategy has never been 'whether to compromise' — it's 'how much to compromise to keep both principles and market.'"— Dawn Vision Editorial

But this compromise carries different weight. Previous compromises were all "peripheral" — data storage, payments, content moderation. This time is different. AI is the core entry point for the next generation of computing. Handing part of that core entry point to third-party suppliers is rare in Apple's history.

It also demonstrates one thing from the side: the China market is too important — important enough for Apple to concede on core strategy. Apple has hundreds of millions of iPhone users in China, and Chinese supply chains support over 90% of Apple's production capacity. If missing AI features reduces Chinese users' upgrade intent, the loss can't be recouped by saving a bit on R&D costs.

See you tomorrow.

pull_quote.text_en: Apple's China strategy has never been 'whether to compromise' — it's 'how much to compromise to keep both principles and market.'

搜狐 - Roblox Build上线！2026年AI游戏创作革命来了

briefs.body_html:
做一款3D游戏需要多久？Roblox的答案是：一句话的时间。

7月28日，Roblox将在移动端正式上线「Build」功能——用户打开App，输入一段文字描述，AI就能生成一款可以直接玩的3D游戏。不是概念图，不是Demo，是有场景、有角色、有交互、有规则的完整小游戏。

这件事的分量，怎么高估都不为过。在Roblox上，以前做一款能玩的游戏，你得会Lua编程、会3D建模、会关卡设计——门槛不低。现在，门槛被AI一脚踹碎了。

从「做游戏」到「描述游戏」，中间隔了多少创作者

Roblox现在有多少创作者？官方数字是几百万。听着很多，但你想想Roblox的月活用户——超过7亿。几百万创作者对7亿用户，比例大概是1:1000。

为什么这么少？因为做游戏难啊。你得学编程、得学建模、得懂游戏设计，还要有时间反复调试。绝大多数玩家就是「玩游戏的」，不是「做游戏的」。

Build功能出来之后，这个比例会变成多少？没人知道，但肯定不是1:1000了。可能是1:100，可能是1:10，甚至可能更高——当创作的门槛降到零，每个人都是创作者。

这不是第一次发生这种事了。当年YouTube把视频创作的门槛从「会用专业摄像机+剪辑软件」降到「有个手机就行」，结果是创作者数量爆炸式增长，内容生态彻底变了天。Roblox现在做的，就是游戏界的YouTube时刻。

但AI生成的游戏，好玩吗？

一个必须面对的问题：AI生成的游戏，质量到底怎么样？

从目前泄露的测试视频来看，Build生成的游戏还比较简单——基本是跑酷、解谜、收集类的小游戏，场景复杂度有限，玩法也比较单一。跟专业团队做的Roblox游戏比，差得还远。

但这重要吗？不重要。因为大多数人做游戏不是为了做爆款，是为了跟朋友分享。你给你女朋友做一个「一起闯关收集爱心」的小游戏，哪怕画面粗糙、玩法简单，她也会觉得你浪漫。你给你的同学做一个「考试答题闯关」的游戏，哪怕逻辑简单，大家也会玩得很嗨。

"AI生成的内容不需要完美，只需要够特别——特别到你愿意分享给朋友。"—— 一位UGC平台产品经理

而且质量会快速提升的。今天生成的是简单跑酷，明年生成的可能就是完整的RPG了。AI的进步速度，比人类学习编程的速度快太多了。

真正值得关注的不是「AI能不能做出好游戏」，而是「当每个人都能做游戏，平台上会出现什么样的新内容生态」。是垃圾内容泛滥？还是百花齐放？是头部效应更强？还是长尾更丰富？这些问题，只有等Build真正上线之后才能有答案。

但有一件事是确定的：游戏创作的民主化，已经不可逆了。

明天见。
briefs.body_html_en:
How long does it take to make a 3D game? Roblox's answer: as long as it takes to type a sentence.

On July 28, Roblox will officially launch its "Build" feature on mobile — users open the app, type a text description, and AI generates a fully playable 3D game. Not concept art, not a demo — a complete mini-game with environments, characters, interactions, and rules.

The significance of this can't be overstated. On Roblox, making a playable game used to require knowing Lua programming, 3D modeling, and level design — a high barrier. Now, AI has kicked that barrier to pieces.

From "Making Games" to "Describing Games" — How Many Creators Are in Between?

How many creators does Roblox have right now? The official number is a few million. Sounds like a lot — until you consider Roblox's monthly active users: over 700 million. A few million creators to 700 million users — roughly a 1:1000 ratio.

Why so few? Because making games is hard. You have to learn programming, learn modeling, understand game design, and have time to iterate and debug. Most players are just "people who play games," not "people who make games."

After Build launches, what will that ratio become? Nobody knows — but it definitely won't be 1:1000. Maybe 1:100, maybe 1:10, maybe even higher. When the creation barrier drops to zero, everyone is a creator.

This isn't the first time this has happened. When YouTube lowered video creation from "professional camera + editing software" to "just a phone," the result was an explosion in creator count and a complete transformation of the content ecosystem. What Roblox is doing now is the YouTube moment for gaming.

But Are AI-Generated Games Any Fun?

A question that must be faced: how good are AI-generated games, really?

From leaked test videos so far, Build generates relatively simple games — basically parkour, puzzle, and collection-type mini-games, with limited scene complexity and straightforward gameplay. Compared to games made by professional Roblox teams, there's still a big gap.

But does that matter? No. Because most people don't make games to create hits — they make them to share with friends. You make a "collect hearts together" game for your girlfriend — even if the graphics are rough and gameplay is simple, she'll think it's romantic. You make an "exam question闯关" game for your classmates — even if the logic is basic, everyone will have a blast playing it.

"AI-generated content doesn't need to be perfect — it just needs to be special enough that you want to share it with friends."— A UGC platform product manager

And quality will improve fast. Today it generates simple parkour. Next year it might generate full RPGs. AI progresses much faster than humans learn to code.

What's really worth watching isn't "can AI make good games" — it's "when everyone can make games, what kind of new content ecosystem emerges on the platform." Will garbage content flood everything? Or will we see a thousand flowers bloom? Will the head get bigger or the long tail richer? These questions can only be answered after Build actually launches.

But one thing is certain: the democratization of game creation is irreversible.

See you tomorrow.

36氪 - 蚂蚁集团WAIC展示面向智能体商业的三层AI布局

briefs.body_html:
B2B的下一个形态，不是人跟人做生意，是AI跟AI做生意。

7月17日WAIC现场，阿里1688宣布将在本月底推出UTP通用交易协议（UniversalTradeProtocol）。这个协议的目标很宏大：为A2A（Agent to Agent）时代的全球贸易，建立一套统一的交易基础设施。

什么意思？就是让买家的AI Agent直接跟工厂的AI Agent对话、询价、比价、下单、支付——整个交易流程不需要人类介入。以前是采购经理在1688上搜商品、问价格、谈条件；以后是你的采购Agent自动对接上百家供应商的Agent，几秒钟就完成比价和谈判。

为什么需要一个协议？没有标准，AI之间也鸡同鸭讲

很多人可能会问：AI不是很聪明吗？让两个AI直接聊不就行了？为什么还要专门做个协议？

因为没有统一标准的话，两个Agent根本没法高效沟通。

想象一下：你是做服装的，你的采购Agent说「我要1000件纯棉T恤」；对面工厂的销售Agent说「我们做针织衫的，MOQ是500件，交货期15天」。两个AI说的都是中文，但术语不一样、单位不一样、字段不一样——你说的「纯棉」是95%棉还是100%棉？我说的「交货期」是从打样开始还是从付款开始？

没有统一协议的话，两个Agent就得花大量时间「对齐认知」——就像两个人来自不同行业，鸡同鸭讲，聊半天才能搞清楚对方在说什么。效率太低了。

UTP就是来解决这个问题的。它定义了商品描述、价格、库存、交货期、支付方式、售后条款等标准化字段，所有接入UTP的Agent都用同一套语言说话。这样两个Agent一碰面，几秒钟就能完成询价和比价，直接进入决策环节。

谁最慌？中间商和贸易公司

UTP如果真能跑起来，受冲击最大的不是工厂，也不是采购方——是中间的贸易商和经销商。

传统B2B贸易里，贸易商的核心价值是什么？是信息差——我知道哪家工厂好、哪家便宜、哪家靠谱，你不知道，所以你找我。但在A2A时代，Agent可以一秒钟对接上百家工厂，所有价格、交期、质量数据一目了然，信息差被抹平了。

而且AI谈判比人狠多了。采购经理磨半天嘴皮子砍下来5个点，Agent可能几秒钟就把10家供应商的底价都摸出来了。中间商以前靠「信息差+人情」吃饭，这两样在AI时代都不好使了。

"每一次基础设施的标准化，都会干掉一层中间商。UTP也不会例外。"—— Dawn Vision编辑部判断

当然，不是所有中间商都会死。那些有供应链整合能力的、能提供质量管控和增值服务的、有资金和账期能力的贸易商，还是会活得很好。但那些纯靠信息差、左手倒右手的「搬砖型」贸易商，日子会越来越难过。

UTP现在还只是阿里1688一家在推，能不能成为行业标准还不好说——毕竟腾讯、京东、拼多多都有自己的B2B业务，谁也不想用对手的标准。但方向是确定的：A2A交易一定会来，统一协议也一定会有。早来晚来而已。

明天见。
briefs.body_html_en:
The next form of B2B isn't humans doing business with humans — it's AI doing business with AI.

At WAIC on July 17, Alibaba 1688 announced it will launch the UTP Universal Trade Protocol at the end of this month. The protocol has an ambitious goal: to build unified trade infrastructure for global commerce in the A2A (Agent to Agent) era.

What does that mean? It means your purchasing AI agent directly talks to the factory's sales agent — inquires, compares prices, places orders, processes payments — the entire trade flow without human involvement. Previously, a procurement manager searched for products, asked for prices, negotiated terms on 1688. Going forward, your purchasing agent automatically connects with agents from hundreds of suppliers and completes price comparison and negotiation in seconds.

Why a Protocol? Without Standards, Even AIs Talk Past Each Other

Many people might ask: aren't AIs smart? Can't two AIs just chat? Why do we need a special protocol?

Because without unified standards, two agents simply can't communicate efficiently.

Imagine this: you're in apparel, your purchasing agent says "I need 1000 pure cotton t-shirts"; the factory's sales agent says "we make knitwear, MOQ 500 pieces, delivery time 15 days." Both AIs are speaking Chinese, but terminology differs, units differ, data fields differ — when you say "pure cotton," do you mean 95% cotton or 100%? When I say "delivery time," is that from sample approval or from payment?

Without a unified protocol, two agents spend massive amounts of time "aligning understanding" — just like two people from different industries talking past each other, taking forever to figure out what the other means. Way too inefficient.

UTP solves this. It defines standardized fields for product descriptions, pricing, inventory, delivery times, payment methods, after-sales terms — all agents connected to UTP speak the same language. So when two agents meet, they complete inquiry and price comparison in seconds and go straight to decision-making.

Who's Most Worried? Middlemen and Trading Companies

If UTP actually takes off, the group hit hardest won't be factories or buyers — it'll be trading companies and distributors in the middle.

In traditional B2B trade, what's the core value of traders? Information asymmetry — I know which factories are good, which are cheap, which are reliable, and you don't, so you come to me. But in the A2A era, agents can connect with hundreds of factories in one second — all prices, delivery times, quality data is transparent. Information asymmetry gets erased.

And AI negotiates harder than humans. A procurement manager haggles for half a day to get 5% off. An agent might extract floor prices from 10 suppliers in seconds. Traders who used to survive on "information gap + relationships" — neither of those works well in the AI era.

"Every time infrastructure gets standardized, a layer of middlemen gets eliminated. UTP won't be an exception."— Dawn Vision Editorial Assessment

Of course, not all middlemen will die. Those with supply chain integration capabilities, those who provide quality control and value-added services, those with capital and credit term capabilities — they'll still do fine. But "brick-moving" traders who purely arbitrage information gaps, buying low and selling high — their days are numbered.

UTP is still being pushed by just Alibaba 1688 right now. Whether it becomes an industry standard remains to be seen — Tencent, JD, Pinduoduo all have their own B2B businesses, and nobody wants to use a competitor's standard. But the direction is certain: A2A commerce is coming, and a unified protocol will come too. It's just a matter of when.

See you tomorrow.

sources.text: 36氪 - 曙光8000亮相WAIC 2026，单个计算单元算力密度提升20倍
量子位 - WAIC 2026镇馆之宝公布

briefs.body_html:
国产算力的硬实力，又往上跳了一级。

7月17日WAIC 2026首日，曙光8000（登峰）以真机形态首次公开亮相，并入选大会「镇馆之宝」。这台机器有两个关键数字：单计算单元算力密度提升20倍，自研scaleFabric互连技术支持十万卡规模稳定互连。

20倍是什么概念？就是同样大小的一个机柜，以前装的算力现在能装20倍。以前需要一整个数据中心的算力，现在一个机房就搞定了。这不仅仅是「更强」，是部署成本和运维复杂度的指数级下降。

为什么算力密度这么重要？不是芯片越多越好吗

很多人以为算力就是堆芯片——卡越多越牛逼。其实完全不是这么回事。

大模型训练不是一堆卡各自为战，是成千上万张卡要协同工作。卡和卡之间要通信、要同步、要传数据。如果互连速度不够快，大部分卡都在等数据，利用率上不去，堆再多卡也没用。

这就是为什么「算力密度」和「互连规模」这两个指标比「单卡算力」更重要。单卡算力再强，如果连不起来、利用率低，整体性能就要打折扣。曙光8000的20倍密度提升，意味着同样的空间里能塞更多卡，而且卡之间的通信距离更短、延迟更低——整体利用率上去了，实际有效算力的提升可能远不止20倍。

十万卡互连这个指标更吓人。目前国内能做到万卡稳定训练的系统就不多，曙光直接把规模拉到了十万级。这意味着什么？意味着可以用国产算力训练超大规模模型了——不再是几千卡、万卡级别的「小打小闹」，是真正能跟海外正面刚的超大规模训练集群。

从「能用」到「好用」，国产算力的关键一跃

曙光8000最值得关注的，不是某个单一指标的突破，而是体系化的成熟度。

前两年国产算力的状态是什么样的？芯片有了，但软件栈不好用、框架适配不全、运维工具缺失、故障定位困难。客户买回去，不是插上电就能用，得有一个团队专门调优、排错、适配。说白了，就是「能用但不好用」。

曙光8000展示的是另一番景象：全精度支持（FP64到INT8）、300余项重点应用适配、覆盖20余个科研与产业领域、自研互连技术。这些东西加在一起，说的是一件事：国产算力正在从「能用」迈向「好用」。

"国产算力的竞争，已经从『有没有芯片』变成了『整系统好不好用』。单点突破没用，体系化能力才是壁垒。"—— 一位算力行业从业者

当然，跟NVIDIA比还有差距。CUDA生态的厚度、H100/H200的单卡性能、InfiniBand的成熟度——这些不是一朝一夕能追上的。但差距在快速缩小，这是确定的。

而且国产算力有一个海外比不了的优势：供应链安全。在地缘政治风险越来越高的今天，「不被卡脖子」本身就是一种核心竞争力。哪怕性能差一点、价格贵一点，只要稳定供货、不被断供，就有客户愿意买单。

曙光8000不是终点，是国产算力进入新阶段的一个标志。接下来的竞争，会从「能不能做出来」转向「能不能规模化交付」「能不能让客户真正用起来」。

明天见。
briefs.body_html_en:
Domestic Chinese compute infrastructure just jumped another level.

On the first day of WAIC 2026, July 17, the Sugon 8000 (Dengfeng / "Peak Climber") made its first public physical appearance and was selected as one of the conference's "hall of fame" exhibits. This machine has two key numbers: 20x compute density per unit, and proprietary scaleFabric interconnect technology supporting stable 100,000-card scale interconnect.

What does 20x mean? In a cabinet of the same size, you can now fit 20 times the compute. What used to require an entire data center now fits in a single machine room. This isn't just "stronger" — it's an exponential drop in deployment cost and operational complexity.

Why Does Compute Density Matter So Much? Isn't More Chips Always Better?

Many people think compute is just stacking chips — more cards = more power. That's not how it works at all.

Large model training isn't thousands of cards working independently — it's tens of thousands of cards that must collaborate. Cards need to communicate, synchronize, transfer data. If interconnect speed isn't fast enough, most cards are waiting for data, utilization stays low, and stacking more cards does nothing.

That's why "compute density" and "interconnect scale" are more important metrics than "single-card performance." However strong a single card is, if you can't connect them well and utilization is low, overall performance gets discounted. Sugon 8000's 20x density improvement means you can fit more cards in the same space, and communication distances between cards are shorter with lower latency — overall utilization goes up, and actual effective compute improvement might be way more than 20x.

The 100,000-card interconnect figure is even more staggering. Right now, not many domestic systems can do stable 10,000-card training — Sugon is pulling the scale straight to 100,000. What does that mean? It means you can train ultra-large-scale models on domestic compute — no longer a few thousand or ten thousand cards of "small potatoes," but truly large-scale training clusters that can go head-to-head with overseas.

From "Usable" to "Good" — Domestic Compute's Critical Leap

What's most noteworthy about Sugon 8000 isn't any single metric breakthrough — it's systematic maturity.

What was the state of domestic compute a couple years ago? Chips existed, but the software stack was rough, framework adaptation was incomplete, ops tools were missing, fault localization was hard. Customers who bought them couldn't just plug them in — they needed dedicated teams for tuning, debugging, adaptation. Plainly put: "usable, but not good."

Sugon 8000 shows a different picture: full precision support (FP64 to INT8), 300+ key application adaptations, coverage across 20+ research and industry fields, proprietary interconnect technology. Put together, all of this says one thing: domestic compute is moving from "usable" to "good".

"The competition in domestic compute has shifted from 'do we have chips' to 'is the whole system good to use.' Single-point breakthroughs don't matter — systematic capability is the moat."— A compute industry practitioner

Of course, there's still a gap with NVIDIA. The depth of the CUDA ecosystem, single-card performance of H100/H200, maturity of InfiniBand — these can't be caught up overnight. But the gap is narrowing fast, that much is certain.

And domestic compute has one advantage overseas can't match: supply chain security. In an era of rising geopolitical risk, "not being choked off" is itself a core competency. Even if performance is a bit lower and price a bit higher, as long as supply is stable and you can't get cut off, there are customers willing to pay.

Sugon 8000 isn't the finish line — it's a marker that domestic compute has entered a new phase. The next phase of competition shifts from "can we build it" to "can we deliver at scale" and "can customers actually use it productively."

See you tomorrow.

36氪 - 阿里1688推出UTP通用交易协议

briefs.body_html:
1亿用户。日均1000万次咨询。这不是某个C端聊天机器人的数据，是一个健康AI Agent的成绩单。

WAIC 2026上，蚂蚁集团首次完整展示了面向智能体商业时代的三层AI布局：AI应用层、智能体商业生态层、技术基座层。其中最亮眼的数字来自应用层——健康AI「阿福」用户数已突破1亿，日均处理超过1000万次健康咨询。

1亿用户是什么概念？很多大模型公司全产品加起来都没这么多用户。而且这1亿不是「注册了但不用」的僵尸用户，是每天都在问健康问题的活跃用户。这可能是目前国内用户量最大的垂直AI Agent。

三层布局：从技术到生态到应用，蚂蚁想做智能体时代的「操作系统」

蚂蚁的三层布局，思路很清晰，野心也很大。

最上层是AI应用层——自己下场做标杆产品。健康AI「阿福」、AI版支付宝「阿宝」，这些都是蚂蚁自己做的Agent。为什么自己做？因为要打样——告诉合作伙伴和开发者，「智能体应该这么做，能做到这个效果」。同时也积累用户、数据和场景理解。

中间层是智能体商业生态层——这是蚂蚁真正的野心所在。AI支付已支持3亿笔智能体支付，适配了95%的通用智能体框架。什么意思？就是不管你用什么框架做的Agent，只要接入了支付宝的AI支付能力，就能自动完成支付。这就像智能体时代的「支付基础设施」——就像当年移动支付时代的支付宝一样，只不过这次服务的对象从人变成了Agent。

最底层是技术基座层——百灵大模型、灵波具身智能、OceanBase AI数据库、安全可信能力。这些是支撑上面两层的基础。蚂蚁不跟别人比单模型跑分，比的是「模型+数据+场景+支付」的综合能力。

三层叠在一起，你会发现蚂蚁想做的不是某一个爆款Agent，而是智能体时代的「操作系统」——你做Agent可以用我的模型、接我的支付、跑在我的生态里。就像Windows时代，微软自己也做Office，但更重要的是所有软件都跑在Windows上。

为什么健康AI先跑出来？场景选对了，事半功倍

阿福能跑到1亿用户，不是偶然的。健康这个场景，天生适合AI Agent。

第一，需求高频且普遍。每个人都会有健康疑问——「这个症状要不要去医院？」「这个药有没有副作用？」「体检报告上这个指标偏高是什么意思？」这些问题不大不小，去医院嫌麻烦，上网搜怕不靠谱，问AI正好。

第二，AI能真正帮上忙。健康咨询不是什么高难度的医疗诊断，就是信息整合和科普——把专业的医学知识用普通人能听懂的话讲出来。这件事AI特别擅长——知识量比任何一个医生都大，而且24小时在线，永远耐心。

第三，有支付宝的流量入口。很多AI产品的最大难题是获客——你做得再好，没人知道也白搭。但阿福不一样，它长在支付宝里，支付宝有几亿用户。只要产品体验过得去，用户获取成本几乎为零。

"Agent创业最大的误区：总想着做通用Agent，却忽略了垂直场景的真实需求。先在一个场景跑通1亿用户，比做一个什么都能做但没人用的通用Agent有价值得多。"—— Dawn Vision编辑部

阿福的1亿用户，给整个行业提了个醒：别总想着做「下一个ChatGPT」，先找一个真实的、高频的、有流量入口的垂直场景，把它做深做透。用户量上来了，数据就有了；数据有了，模型就更强了；模型更强了，用户体验就更好了——这是一个正向循环。

智能体的竞争，从来不比谁更「通用」，比谁更「有用」。

明天见。
briefs.body_html_en:
100 million users. 10 million daily consultations. This isn't data from some consumer chatbot — it's a report card for a health AI agent.

At WAIC 2026, Ant Group for the first time fully showcased its three-layer AI architecture for the agent business era: AI application layer, agent business ecosystem layer, and technology foundation layer. The most eye-catching number comes from the application layer — health AI "A Fu" has surpassed 100 million users, handling over 10 million health consultations daily.

What does 100 million users mean? Many LLM companies don't have that many users across all their products combined. And these 100 million aren't zombie users who "signed up but never use" — they're active users asking health questions every day. This is likely China's largest vertical AI agent by user count.

Three Layers: From Tech to Ecosystem to Apps — Ant Wants to Be the 'OS' of the Agent Era

Ant's three-layer strategy is clear in its thinking — and ambitious in its scope.

Top layer: AI application layer — building flagship products themselves. Health AI "A Fu," AI-powered Alipay "A Bao" — these are agents Ant built itself. Why build your own? To set the example — showing partners and developers "this is how agents should be built, and this is what they can achieve." It also accumulates users, data, and scenario understanding.

Middle layer: agent business ecosystem layer — this is where Ant's real ambition lies. AI payment already supports 300 million agent transactions, adapted to 95% of common agent frameworks. What does that mean? No matter what framework you use to build your agent, as long as it integrates Alipay's AI payment capabilities, it can automatically complete payments. It's like "payment infrastructure" for the agent era — just like Alipay in the mobile payment era, except this time it's serving agents instead of people.

Bottom layer: technology foundation — Bailin LLM, Lingbo embodied AI, OceanBase AI database, trusted security capabilities. These are the foundation supporting the two layers above. Ant doesn't compete on single-model benchmarks — it competes on the combined capability of "model + data + scenario + payment."

Stack all three layers together and you see what Ant really wants to be — not just another hit agent, but the "operating system" of the agent era. You build agents using my models, connecting to my payment, running in my ecosystem. Just like in the Windows era — Microsoft made Office too, but what mattered more was that all software ran on Windows.

Why Did Health AI Break Out First? Pick the Right Scenario and Everything Gets Easier

A Fu reaching 100 million users isn't an accident. The health scenario is天生 (naturally) suited for AI agents.

First: high-frequency, universal demand. Everyone has health questions — "should I go to the hospital for this symptom?" "does this medicine have side effects?" "what does this high indicator on my physical exam mean?" These questions aren't serious enough for a hospital visit, but searching online feels unreliable — asking AI is perfect.

Second: AI can actually help. Health consultation isn't high-stakes medical diagnosis — it's information synthesis and科普 (health education): translating professional medical knowledge into language ordinary people understand. This is something AI excels at — it has more knowledge than any single doctor, it's available 24/7, and it's always patient.

Third: Alipay's traffic entry point. The biggest challenge for many AI products is user acquisition — however good your product is, it's useless if nobody knows about it. But A Fu is different — it lives inside Alipay, and Alipay has hundreds of millions of users. As long as the product experience is decent, user acquisition cost is essentially zero.

"The biggest mistake in agent startups: always trying to build general agents while ignoring real demand in vertical scenarios. Getting 100M users in one scenario first is way more valuable than building a general agent that does everything but nobody uses."— Dawn Vision Editorial

A Fu's 100 million users is a wake-up call for the entire industry: stop trying to build "the next ChatGPT." First find a real, high-frequency vertical scenario with a traffic entry point, and go deep on it. When user volume goes up, you get data; with data, your model gets better; with a better model, user experience improves — it's a virtuous cycle.

The competition in agents is never about who's more "general." It's about who's more "useful."

See you tomorrow.

TechCrunch AI - EU Google antitrust ruling

briefs.body_html:
欧盟的反垄断大棒，这次精准地砸在了Google的AI命门上。

7月16日，欧盟根据数字市场法（DMA）作出两项最新裁决：Google必须向竞争对手开放Android操作系统和Google搜索的关键部分，允许竞品AI助手和搜索引擎接入。简单说就是：你的手机是Android的，但你可以用别的AI助手，而且Google不能给它使绊子。

这不是普通的罚款——罚款Google交得起，而且交完还能继续玩。这次的裁决是结构性的——要从根本上改变Google在AI时代的竞争优势。

为什么是现在？AI改变了反垄断的游戏规则

欧盟反垄断盯Google不是一天两天了——搜索、广告、Android，罚了一轮又一轮。但之前的反垄断，针对的都是上一个时代的市场：搜索市场、移动操作系统市场、广告市场。

这次不一样。这次的裁决，核心关键词是AI助手的互操作性。欧盟认为，Google利用Android和搜索的垄断地位，把自己的AI助手（Gemini）预装在每一部Android手机上、放在搜索结果的最顶部，让竞争对手的AI助手根本没有公平竞争的机会。

这个逻辑很关键。以前反垄断查的是「你在这个市场有没有垄断」，现在查的是「你会不会用这个市场的垄断地位，去拿下一个市场的垄断」。AI助手是下一代计算入口——如果Google用Android和搜索的优势，把Gemini的入口占死了，那以后所有的AI服务都得看Google的脸色。

这就是为什么欧盟要抢在AI市场还没完全定型的时候出手——等格局定了再反，就晚了。

Google怎么应对？「合规」但「不认输」

面对欧盟的裁决，Google的回应很官方：「我们将仔细评估并遵守这些要求，但我们也会考虑上诉。」翻译成人话就是：我先按你说的做，但我不服，我要继续打官司。

这是科技巨头应对监管的标准操作：一边合规一边上诉，拖个三五年，说不定市场环境都变了。而且Google有的是办法「软抵制」——你让我开放接口是吧？我开放，但接口做得特别难用、文档写得特别烂、Bug特别多、出了问题没人管。竞争对手接是能接上，但体验差一大截，用户还是会用回Gemini。

"反垄断的历史就是一部猫鼠游戏史——监管出一招，巨头有一百种方法绕过去。但每次绕过去，垄断的形式就变一次。"—— 一位科技政策研究者

但不管怎么说，这对其他AI助手公司来说都是好事。尤其是那些欧洲本土的AI公司——以前它们想碰Android用户，比登天还难。现在至少有了一条通路，哪怕这条路不好走，也比没有强。

更大的意义在于示范效应。欧盟开了这个头，其他国家和地区会不会跟进？英国、日本、韩国，甚至美国自己——如果AI助手的入口被一两家公司垄断，各国监管都不会坐视不管。

AI时代的反垄断，才刚刚开始。Google只是第一个靶子，绝不会是最后一个。Apple、Microsoft、Amazon、Meta……这些手里有「入口」的公司，一个都跑不掉。

明天见。
briefs.body_html_en:
The EU's antitrust hammer just landed precisely on Google's AI pressure point.

On July 16, the EU issued two new rulings under the Digital Markets Act (DMA): Google must open key parts of Android and Google Search to competitors, allowing rival AI assistants and search engines access. Put simply: your phone runs Android, but you can use a different AI assistant — and Google can't sabotage it.

This isn't a normal fine — Google can pay fines and keep playing. This ruling is structural — it aims to fundamentally change Google's competitive advantage in the AI era.

Why Now? AI Changes the Antitrust Game

EU antitrust hasn't just discovered Google overnight — they've gone after search, ads, Android, fined them round after round. But previous antitrust targeted markets of the previous era: search, mobile OS, advertising.

This time is different. The core keyword of this ruling is AI assistant interoperability. The EU argues that Google is using its dominant position in Android and search to pre-install its own AI assistant (Gemini) on every Android phone and place it at the top of search results — giving competing AI assistants no chance to compete fairly.

This logic is critical. Previously, antitrust investigated "do you have a monopoly in this market." Now it's investigating "are you using your monopoly in this market to secure a monopoly in the next one." AI assistants are the next computing entry point — if Google uses Android and search advantages to lock up the Gemini entry point, then all AI services going forward will have to play by Google's rules.

That's why the EU is acting now, before the AI market fully solidifies — act after the格局 (landscape) is set and it's too late.

How Google Responds: 'Complying' But 'Not Conceding'

Faced with the EU ruling, Google's response was very official: "We will carefully evaluate and comply with these requirements, but we are also considering an appeal." Translated from corporate speak: we'll do what you say for now, but we don't accept it, and we'll keep fighting in court.

This is standard operating procedure for tech giants facing regulation: comply while appealing, drag it out for three to five years, and by then the market environment might have changed. And Google has plenty of ways to "softly resist" — you want open APIs? Fine, we'll open them, but make them especially hard to use, write terrible documentation, fill them with bugs, and have nobody to call when things break. Competitors can connect — but the experience will be so much worse that users will just go back to Gemini.

"The history of antitrust is a history of cat-and-mouse — regulators make one move, and giants have a hundred ways around it. But each time they go around, the form of monopoly changes."— A tech policy researcher

Either way, this is good news for other AI assistant companies. Especially European domestic AI companies — previously, getting access to Android users was harder than climbing to the sky. Now there's at least a path. Even if the path is rough, it's better than nothing.

The bigger significance is the demonstration effect. The EU has set this precedent — will other countries and regions follow? The UK, Japan, Korea, even the US itself — if the AI assistant entry point gets monopolized by one or two companies, no regulator will sit idly by.

AI-era antitrust is just getting started. Google is just the first target — it definitely won't be the last. Apple, Microsoft, Amazon, Meta… every company that controls an "entry point" won't escape scrutiny.

See you tomorrow.

sources.text: 量子位 - 一句「哈哈」引发的苹果OpenAI窃密大战
TechCrunch - Amid hardware legal battle, OpenAI releases a $230 keyboard for Codex

cao.body_html:
朋友们，今天AI圈的瓜，甜到我可以就着它吃三碗饭。

事情是这样的：苹果把OpenAI告了，说OpenAI挖了苹果的员工，偷了苹果的AI硬件商业机密。这个事儿本身不稀奇——硅谷公司之间互相挖人打官司，家常便饭。但离奇的是证据。

苹果在法庭文件里说：那个从苹果跳到OpenAI的员工，在离职前下载了大量机密文件。然后，在跟OpenAI的人通话的时候，他说了一句「哈哈」（ha ha）。苹果认为——这声「哈哈」是「对偷窃行为沾沾自喜的表现」。

没错，你没看错。苹果的核心证据之一，是对方笑了一声。

因为一声笑就起诉？苹果是不是太敏感了

很多人第一反应：苹果是不是魔怔了？人家笑一声怎么了？说不定就是听到个笑话呢？

但你品，你细品。苹果是什么公司？以保密文化著称的公司，内部保密级别堪比情报机构。它的法务团队是吃素的吗？会因为一声笑就随便起诉？不可能。苹果手里肯定还有别的东西——只是目前公开的文件里，最有梗的就是这个「哈哈」。

而且这个「哈哈」的语境很关键。根据苹果的说法，通话内容是在讨论苹果的机密项目。讨论机密项目的时候突然「哈哈」——你说这是正常的笑？还是「嘿嘿我们偷到了」的那种笑？

当然，OpenAI的说法完全不一样：「那就是随口一笑，你们想太多了。」OpenAI说，那个员工是在聊工作的时候觉得某件事挺好笑，就笑了一声，根本不是什么「沾沾自喜」。苹果这是欲加之罪，何患无辞。

两边各执一词，真相是什么？只有当事人知道。但这不重要——重要的是，这个瓜太好吃了。

AI硬件大战，已经打到了谍战片的程度

这件事背后真正值得聊的，不是谁对谁错，是AI硬件赛道到底有多卷。

以前AI公司之间抢人，抢的是算法工程师、研究员。现在抢的是什么？硬件工程师、芯片设计师、产品经理。为什么？因为AI的下一个战场不是软件，是硬件——AI手机、AI眼镜、AI耳机、AI键盘……每家都在搞，都在抢人，都在偷偷摸摸搞秘密项目。

你想想那个画面：苹果在做AI硬件，OpenAI也在做AI硬件，两边都想抢对方的人才，都想知道对方在做什么。挖走一个关键员工，就等于知道了对方的路线图、时间表、技术方案。这比商业间谍还高效。

所以苹果急啊。不急才怪。自己辛辛苦苦做了好几年的秘密项目，核心员工被挖走了，项目机密可能全泄露了。换谁谁不急？

但急归急，拿一句「哈哈」当证据这事，确实有点……怎么说呢，有失硅谷第一科技公司的体面。感觉就像两口子吵架，一方说「你笑了！你心虚了！你就是出轨了！」——虽然你可能真的出轨了，但拿「笑了」当证据，说服力确实差点意思。

实用提醒

好了，槽点归槽点，最后给大家三条实用提醒：

第一，重要的话别在电话里说。尤其是涉及商业机密的，能线下聊就线下聊，能当面说就当面说。电话录音、通话记录，分分钟呈堂证供。

第二，离职前别乱下载文件。不管你觉得那些文件是你自己做的还是怎么样，公司电脑里的东西都是公司的。离职前大规模下载文件，跳进黄河都洗不清。

第三，讨论敏感话题的时候别笑。真的，你可能只是随口一笑，但对方的录音笔录下来，配上上下文，就能解读出一万种意思。尤其是你对面坐的是苹果的法务团队。

今天就槽到这里，明天继续。
cao.body_html_en:
Friends, today's AI industry drama is so juicy I could eat three bowls of rice with it.

Here's what happened: Apple sued OpenAI, saying OpenAI poached Apple employees and stole Apple's AI hardware trade secrets. This part isn't surprising — Silicon Valley companies poaching each other and suing over it is par for the course. What's bizarre is the evidence.

Apple said in court filings that the employee who jumped from Apple to OpenAI downloaded大量 confidential files before leaving. Then, during a call with OpenAI people, he said "ha ha." Apple believes — that "ha ha" was "a display of smug satisfaction about the theft."

That's right, you read correctly. One of Apple's key pieces of evidence is that the guy laughed.

Suing Over a Laugh? Is Apple Being Too Sensitive?

Many people's first reaction: has Apple lost it? The guy laughed — so what? Maybe he just heard a joke?

But think about it, really think. What kind of company is Apple? A company famous for its secrecy culture, internal security levels comparable to an intelligence agency. Is its legal team incompetent? Would they sue over a single laugh? No way. Apple definitely has other stuff — it's just that in the currently public filings, the "ha ha" is the most meme-worthy part.

And the context of that "ha ha" matters. According to Apple, the call was about discussing Apple's confidential projects. Suddenly laughing in the middle of discussing confidential projects — is that a normal laugh? Or the "heh heh we stole it" kind of laugh?

Of course, OpenAI's side says something completely different: "It was just a casual laugh, you're overthinking this." OpenAI says the employee just found something funny in the work conversation and laughed — no "smug satisfaction" about it. Apple is grasping at straws.

Both sides have their stories. What's the truth? Only the people involved know. But that doesn't matter — what matters is, this drama is delicious.

The AI Hardware War Has Reached Spy Thriller Levels

What's really worth talking about behind all this isn't who's right or wrong — it's how cutthroat the AI hardware track is.

Previously, AI companies fought over algorithm engineers and researchers. What are they fighting over now? Hardware engineers, chip designers, product managers. Why? Because the next battlefield for AI isn't software — it's hardware. AI phones, AI glasses, AI earbuds, AI keyboards… everyone's building them, everyone's poaching talent, everyone's working on secret projects behind closed doors.

Picture the scene: Apple's building AI hardware, OpenAI's building AI hardware, both sides want to poach each other's key people, both want to know what the other is working on. Poach one key employee and you essentially get the other side's roadmap, timeline, technical approach. More efficient than a corporate spy.

So of course Apple is frantic. You'd be frantic too. A secret project you've been working on for years, your key employee gets poached, and all your project secrets might be leaked. Who wouldn't be?

But frantic or not, using a "ha ha" as evidence is just… how to put it… beneath the dignity of Silicon Valley's #1 tech company. It feels like a couple fighting where one side goes "you laughed! you're guilty! you cheated!" — even if you actually cheated, using "you laughed" as evidence is a bit weak.

Practical Tips

Alright, jokes aside, here are three practical tips for everyone:

First: don't say important things on the phone. Especially when trade secrets are involved. Talk in person if you can, face to face. Call recordings, call logs — they become evidence in court in minutes.

Second: don't download files recklessly before quitting. Doesn't matter if you think those files are "yours because you made them" — everything on a company computer belongs to the company. Mass-downloading files before leaving? You'll never be able to prove your innocence.

Third: don't laugh when discussing sensitive topics. Seriously, you might just be casually amused, but the other side's recording device captures it and, with the right context, can interpret it ten thousand different ways. Especially when the other side's legal team is Apple's.

That's enough cao for today. More tomorrow.
